query: histopathology and Gleason scoring in prostate cancer

retrieved_docs.title: Prostate Cancer -- Histopathology -- The Gleason Scoring System
retrieved_docs.content: The number of positive biopsies also has a prognostic value. In a study involving 960 patients with intermediate-grade (Gleason 3+4=7) prostate cancer followed for at least 4 years, 86% of patients with less than 34% positive biopsies demonstrated a stable PSA compared to only 11% of patients with more than 50% of positive biopsies.

retrieved_docs.title: Prostate Cancer -- Histopathology -- The Gleason Scoring System
retrieved_docs.content: The Gleason prostate cancer score has proven to be the most reliable and predictive histological grading system over time. Developed initially by pathologist Dr. Donald Gleason in the 1960s, it has stood the test of time and has been universally adopted for all descriptions of prostate cancer. [103]

retrieved_docs.title: Prostate Cancer -- Histopathology -- The Gleason Scoring System
retrieved_docs.content: The Gleason score always contains 2 grades in the form of numbers and then a total score. The predominant Gleason grade pattern is always the first number, 1 to 5, and the second is any secondary or minor pattern, graded 1 to 5. So the absolute best and lowest risk Gleason score is Gleason 1+1=2, and the worst high-grade pathology is Gleason 5+5=10. In real life, these histological extremes are rarely observed. [105]

retrieved_docs.title: Prostate Cancer -- Histopathology -- The Gleason Scoring System
retrieved_docs.content: Cancer volume is another important prognostic parameter, but it is difficult to measure accurately with available technology. Currently, prostatic MRI is the best available tool for estimating tumor volume. [112]

retrieved_docs.title: Prostate Cancer -- Histopathology -- The Gleason Scoring System
retrieved_docs.content: Perineural invasion can help predict extracapsular tumor extension and may be associated with slightly higher tumor aggressiveness. However, studies have conflicting results regarding its clinical usefulness. [113]

retrieved_docs.title: Prostate Cancer -- Histopathology -- The New Gleason Scoring System
retrieved_docs.content: Grade Group 5 (Gleason scores 9 or 10): Lacks gland formation (or with necrosis) with or without poorly formed, fused, or cribriform glands. In clinical practice, Grade Group 1 is histologically considered low-grade, Grade Group 2 is intermediate-grade, and Grade Group 3 or higher is high-grade disease.

retrieved_docs.title: Prostate Cancer -- Histopathology -- The Gleason Scoring System
retrieved_docs.content: If only 1 Gleason grade or pattern is observed, then the Gleason score consists of the same Gleason grade repeated and added together as in Gleason 3+3=6, which happens to be the most commonly found Gleason score. [106] Low-grade tumors are any Gleason score of 3+3=6 or less. [105] Intermediate-grade cancers are classified as having a Gleason score of 3+4=7, meaning that most of the tumor was Gleason grade 3, but a smaller portion was the more aggressive Gleason grade 4. [107] A Gleason score of 4+3=7 or higher is considered high-grade cancer. [105]

retrieved_docs.title: Prostate Cancer -- Histopathology -- The Gleason Scoring System
retrieved_docs.content: The Gleason scoring system is based on the microscopic arrangement, architecture, or pattern of the glands in the prostate rather than on the individual cellular characteristics that define most other cancers. Patterns are graded from 1 to 5, with 1 indicating an almost normal microscopic glandular pattern and appearance and 5 representing the absence of glandular architecture and the presence of only abnormal cancer cells in sheets. [104]

retrieved_docs.title: Gleason Score -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: The Gleason score is essential in determining the prognosis of prostatic malignancies; however, it is not absolute. The Gleason score is subject to interpretation and individual pathologists vary in their evaluations and assessments. Artificial intelligence programs have been developed to assist in more accurate, reliable, and consistent prostate cancer grading but these are not yet in widespread use. [9]

retrieved_docs.title: Prostate Cancer -- Histopathology -- The New Gleason Scoring System
retrieved_docs.content: In 2016, the WHO proposed a new classification system based on clinical experience with the old Gleason scoring system. The update was motivated by findings that clinical outcomes showed minimal differences in lower Gleason scores but varied more significantly in higher grades. The summary of the new Gleason system is as follows: [114] Grade Group 1 (Gleason score ≤6): Only individual, discrete, well-formed glands. Grade Group 2 (Gleason score 3+4=7): Predominantly well-formed glands with a lesser component of poorly-formed, fused, or cribriform glands. Grade Group 3 (Gleason score 4+3=7): Predominantly poorly-formed, fused, or cribriform glands with a lesser component of well-formed glands Grade Group 4 (Gleason score 8): Only poorly-formed, fused, or cribriform glands; predominantly well-formed glands with a lesser component lacking glands; or predominantly lacking glands with a lesser component of well-formed glands.

retrieved_docs.title: Prostate Cancer -- Histopathology -- The Gleason Scoring System
retrieved_docs.content: Although the architecture or pattern described by the Gleason score is a key component in the histological diagnosis of prostate cancer, it is not the sole criterion. For example, prostate-specific membrane antigen (PSMA) is a transmembrane carboxypeptidase that exhibits folate hydrolase activity, which is overexpressed in prostate cancer tissues, indicating the presence of prostate cancer. [108] The presence of neuroendocrine cells and a cribriform pattern are negative prognostic indicators. [109] [110] Other significant microscopic histological features and prognostic indicators of prostate cancer include the following: [111]

retrieved_docs.title: Gleason Score -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Physical exam findings indicating a very hard, extensive prostate, may also indicate more advanced disease and a poorer prognosis. Tumor staging is based on radiographic assessment (evidence of nodal or metastatic disease) and is also used to assess the prognosis. [5] [6] But everything starts with the PSA level for initial cancer screening and the Gleason score as the key, initial prognostic histological classification tool. [4] [24]

retrieved_docs.title: Gleason Score -- Issues of Concern
retrieved_docs.content: In 2014, the International Society of Urological Pathology consensus conference recommended some changes to the Gleason Scoring System. They recommended adding the percentage of Gleason pattern 4 as well as 5 new grades of prostate cancer based on the Gleason Score. These grades would include all Gleason Scores of 6 or less in Grade I, Gleason 3+4=7 in Grade II, Gleason 4+3=7 in Grade III, Gleason 4+4=8 in Grade IV, and all Gleason 9 and 10 in Grade V. [7]

retrieved_docs.title: Gleason Score -- Issues of Concern
retrieved_docs.content: Decipher uses the RNA expression levels of 22 genes. It gives a 5-year risk of metastasis, a 10-year risk of prostate cancer-specific mortality, and the likelihood of finding high-grade cancer on radical prostatectomy. It is useful in men with localized prostate cancer. Like Prolaris, it can be used as a prognostic aid after radical prostatectomy surgery, particularly for those with clinical high-risk findings at surgery or with higher risk histology.

retrieved_docs.title: Gleason Score -- Issues of Concern
retrieved_docs.content: Additional histological risk factors that are recognized as being independent predictors of prostate cancer aggressiveness but are not currently incorporated into the Gleason scoring system include: [12] Presence of any Gleason Grade 5 tissue, even very minor Percentage of Gleason Grade 4 in the specimen Invasive Gleason Grade 4 cribriform pattern and/or intraductal carcinoma

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 4 -- Community 2802
retrieved_docs.content: The prostate (prostata) is the specific anatomic site associated with metastatic prostate cancer (سرطان البروستاتا النَّقيلي), indicating that this malignant disease originates in the prostate gland and has the potential to spread beyond its primary location, a relationship that is essential for accurate diagnosis, staging, and therapeutic planning.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 2 -- Community 1087
retrieved_docs.content: The prostate (prostata) is a key anatomical site within the male genital system and is the focus of specific diagnostic procedures that are applied when the prostate tissue has been partially excised. This organ serves as the anatomic location for metastatic prostate cancer (سرطان البروستاتا النَّقيلي), linking the disease directly to the prostate tissue. In addition, the prostate is identified as the location of a broader category of prostatic disorder (اضطراب بروستاتي), which functions as a parent classification for other male genital disorders. These other male genital disorders are themselves situated in the prostate and are closely associated with screening activities (التحصّي). Together, these relationships highlight the central role of the prostate in both disease manifestation and diagnostic evaluation, emphasizing the importance of targeted procedures and screening in the management of prostate‑related conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 25 -- Level 3 -- Community 2639

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 3 -- Community 2459
retrieved_docs.content: Prostata is identified as the anatomic site associated with سرطان البروستاتا النَّقيلي, indicating that prostate adenocarcinoma originates in the prostate. Recognizing the prostate as the common site for both malignant and non‑malignant conditions highlights its clinical significance for diagnosis, treatment planning, and patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 31 -- Level 3 -- Community 1517
retrieved_docs.content: Transrectal biopsy of prostate and open prostatectomy are both classified under the broader category of عمليات البروستاتا (prostate procedures). As prostate procedures, they represent distinct interventions used in the evaluation and management of prostate disease: the transrectal biopsy of prostate is a diagnostic technique that obtains tissue samples for histologic assessment, while open prostatectomy is a surgical approach that removes prostate tissue to treat conditions such as benign prostatic hyperplasia or localized prostate cancer. Recognizing that both procedures belong to the same overarching group of prostate operations helps clinicians select appropriate diagnostic or therapeutic strategies within the context of prostate health care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 2529
retrieved_docs.content: Diagnostic procedures; male genital refer to examinations and interventions performed on male genital organs, and the prostate (prostata) is a partially excised anatomy that is commonly involved in such diagnostic procedures. The prostate (prostata) is also the anatomic site associated with metastatic prostate cancer (سرطان البروستاتا النَّقيلي). Recognizing that the prostate can be partially removed for diagnostic evaluation and that it serves as the primary location for this malignant disease is essential for accurate diagnosis, staging, and appropriate patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 29 -- Level 3 -- Community 1808
retrieved_docs.content: Genitourinary symptoms and ill‑defined conditions arise within the urinary system and frequently co‑occur with prostatic disorders, seminal calculi, and both localized and metastatic prostate cancer; they are also clinically associated with prostate cancer. The prostate is an organ that belongs to the urinary system and serves as the anatomic site for prostate‑related diseases, including metastatic prostate cancer. Prostatic disorders are situated in the prostate, and other male genital disorders are likewise located in the prostate, representing subcategories of prostatic disorders. These other male genital disorders are linked to screening activities. Together, these relationships emphasize that disturbances of the prostate and associated genitourinary symptoms are central to the presentation, co‑occurrence, and clinical management of prostate disease, particularly cancer, and underscore the importance of appropriate diagnostic screening.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 4 -- Community 2865
retrieved_docs.content: Genitourinary symptoms and ill-defined conditions are frequently observed together with حصاة حالبية, indicating that patients presenting with vague or nonspecific genitourinary complaints may also have ureteral stones. In addition, these genitourinary symptoms and ill-defined conditions are clinically associated with سرطان البروستاتا, suggesting that prostate cancer can manifest with or be accompanied by such nonspecific genitourinary findings. The same category of genitourinary symptoms and ill-defined conditions also co‑occurs with سرطان البروستاتا النَّقيلي, reflecting that metastatic prostate cancer may present with similar ambiguous genitourinary manifestations. Recognizing these overlapping relationships is important for clinicians to consider a broad differential diagnosis, ensure appropriate imaging and laboratory evaluation, and promptly identify underlying urolithiasis or prostate malignancy, including its metastatic form.

Other male genital disorders are categorized under اضطراب بروستاتي, are located in the prostate, and are associated with التحصّي (screening). Understanding these overlapping clinical presentations, anatomical relationships, and therapeutic links supports more comprehensive patient assessment and management in urologic practice.
